coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode strategy for sensing and bio-imaging of endogenous alkaline phosphatase based on the combination of photoinduced electron transfer and hyperchromic effect
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xuemin
Lan, Maojin
Wang, Jian
Guo, Longhua
Lin, Zhenyu
Zhang, Fan
Zhang, Tao
Wu, Cuimin
Qiu, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Benefit from the additional correction of the output signal in dual-mode detection, traditional dual signal readout strategies are performed by constructing the ratiometric fluorescent probe through excitation energy transfer (EET) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). To avoid the complicated modification process and obtain the results rapidly, a simple dual-mode sensing strategy based on the electronic effects of p-nitrophenol (PNP) is described to monitor the activities of alkaline phosphatase (ALP). In the sensing platform, p-nitrophenylphosphate was used as a substrate to produce the PNP using ALP as the catalyst. Due to the PNP possesses negative effect of induction and conjugation, photoinduced electron transfer and hyperchromic effect have been achieved between PNP and polyethyleneimine-protected copper nanoclusters (PEI-Cu NCs), which caused the changes of the fluorescence intensity and UV–visible absorption. The dual-mode signal sensing system showed the satisfactory linear results of ALP from 1 to 100 U/L for fluorescent sensing strategy and 1–70 U/L for the absorption method with a competitive LOD of 0.27 and 0.87 U/L (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). This strategy detected biological ALP in human serum and bio-imaging of endogenous ALP in A549 cells successfully, which verifies a certain potential of the strategy for the practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intracellular ALP
Dual-mode
Bio-imaging
Copper nanoclusters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The determination of human serum samples
Principle of ALP activity assay based on PET and hyperchromic effect
The dual-mode assay of ALP activity
Assay performances for ALP in human serum samples
Cytotoxicity and endogenous ALP activity sensing in living cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of PEI-Cu NCs, the inset is the HRTEM of PEI-Cu NCs. (B) UV–

visible absorption spectra of PEI and PEI-Cu NCs (5 times dilution). (C) Fluorescence spectra of PEI-Cu NCs (200 μL), excitation and emission wavelength are 380 nm and 500 nm, respectively. (D) IR spectra of PEI-Cu NCs (200 μL).

UV–visible absorption spectra of the assistant test to verify the hyperchromic effect of PNP, all the concentration of PEI-Cu NCs, PNPP, PNP, and ALP are 50 μL, 10 mM, 1 mM, and 50 U/L, respectively. Experimental condition: pH (8.0), reaction time (40 min), the concentration of PNPP (10 mM), temperature (37 °C).

(A) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the reaction solutions with blank (red column) and 100 U/L ALP (blue column). Experimental condition: reaction time (40 min), the concentration of PNPP (10 mM), temperature (37 °C). (B) Effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of the reaction solutions containing different activities of ALP, from bottom to top is 1, 10, 30, 50, 100 U/L, respectively. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of PEI-Cu NCs in the absence and presence of ALP, respectively. Experimental condition: pH (8.0), the concentration of PNPP (10 mM), temperature (37 °C). . (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence responses of PEI-Cu NCs upon the addition of various concentrations of ALP. (B) Plots of FL Intensity versus ALP concentration from 0 to 100 U/L (inset: its corresponding linear relationship from 1 to 100 U/L). (C) Absorbance responses of PEI-Cu NCs upon the addition of various concentrations of ALP. (D) Plots of absorbance versus ALP concentration from 0 to 100 U/L (inset: its corresponding linear relationship from 1 to 70 U/L). Experimental condition: pH (8.0), reaction time (40 min), the concentration of PNPP (10 mM), temperature (37 °C).
Confocal fluorescence images of A549 cells incubated with (a) PEI-Cu NCs, and (b) PEI-Cu NCs + PNPP. Experimental condition: PEI-Cu NCs (0.2 x), PNPP (10 mM) Incubation time (24 h), DAPI (1 μg/mL).
Schematic illustration of the dual-mode strategy for the detection of ALP.
Results of the determination of ALP in two human serum samples.

head.ce:title: A dual-mode strategy for sensing and bio-imaging of endogenous alkaline phosphatase based on the combination of photoinduced electron transfer and hyperchromic effect

Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian, 350108, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Benefit from the additional correction of the output signal in dual-mode detection, traditional dual signal readout strategies are performed by constructing the ratiometric fluorescent probe through excitation energy transfer (EET) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). To avoid the complicated modification process and obtain the results rapidly, a simple dual-mode sensing strategy based on the electronic effects of p-nitrophenol (PNP) is described to monitor the activities of alkaline phosphatase (ALP). In the sensing platform, p-nitrophenylphosphate was used as a substrate to produce the PNP using ALP as the catalyst. Due to the PNP possesses negative effect of induction and conjugation, photoinduced electron transfer and hyperchromic effect have been achieved between PNP and polyethyleneimine-protected copper nanoclusters (PEI-Cu NCs), which caused the changes of the fluorescence intensity and UV–visible absorption. The dual-mode signal sensing system showed the satisfactory linear results of ALP from 1 to 100 U/L for fluorescent sensing strategy and 1–70 U/L for the absorption method with a competitive LOD of 0.27 and 0.87 U/L (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). This strategy detected biological ALP in human serum and bio-imaging of endogenous ALP in A549 cells successfully, which verifies a certain potential of the strategy for the practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) as a vital diagnostic indicator in serum for various diseases is used to indicate breast cancer, liver dysfunction, bone disease, and diabetes [1–4]. Thus, the determination of the activity of ALP with sensitivity is of considerable significance to clinical diagnosis. Recently, several methods, for example, fluorophore, electrochemistry, signal amplification, and paper-based sensors have been significantly developed to monitor the ALP activity for achieving high sensitivity and selectivity [5–12]. Although each method has its own characteristics and mental perception for ALP sense, they are usually confronted with shortages such as poor solubility and photostability in water, complex surface modification and synthesis process, high cost, and complicated sensing steps [13–15]. Furthermore, some of the reported works are limited to the application of biological testing because of the poor linear range and sensitivity in normal human serum with 40–160 U/L for adults [6,15,16]. Therefore, exploiting high sensitive and selective ALP sensing platform with simple operation, low toxicity, and cost-effectiveness is still a challenging task.

As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, metal nanoclusters (NCS) have attracted extensive research interest in the field of analysis because of their excellent chemical, physical, optical and electrical properties. These NCs usually consist of only a few to hundreds of metal atoms, and they are similar in size to the Fermi wavelengths of electrons. Thus, it always has size-dependent fluorescence in the range of ultraviolet to near-infrared. These excellent properties have been applied in many areas of analysis and biochemistry [17–25]. Because Cu is easy to obtain, low price, and has been widely used in life, compared with Au and Ag, the study and application of Cu NCs have attracted more and more attention recently. Moreover, Cu, as one of the essential trace elements for the human body, possesses better biocompatibility and lower toxicity [26–28]. Consequently, expanding the application of Cu NCs is a promising and potential task.

Fluorescence and colorimetric assays are the common measurements for the detection of analyzes, which can easily figure out the intensity trends or color changes [29–36]. These dual-mode signal output can be used as self-control, on the one hand, it can greatly counteract the influence of the base of the system, the signal is more stable and less vulnerable to interference; on the other hand, it provides a more effective and reliable way for accurate detection. Recently, in order to design sensitive, selective and accurate sensors, great efforts have been made in designing colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing platforms [37–39]. He et al. [40] designed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on composite CDots-Au NCs for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine. Fu et al. [41] developed a dual-mode fluorescent probe based on the integration of fluorescein and coumarin fluorophores to distinguish cysteine in Hcy and GSH. These works performed diverse insights for analyzing the targets using different materials and methods. However, the dual-mode sensing of the most effective works is achieved by constructing the ratiometric fluorescent probe through excitation energy transfer (EET) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which suffer from complex-modified procedures, time-effectiveness, and transitory stability. Thus, developing a simple, quick and stabilized assay to obtain the dual signal for accurate detection is an attractive issue.

In this work, a new kind of dual-mode signal assay based on hyperchromic effect and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) via polyethyleneimine-protected copper nanoclusters (PEI-Cu NCs) is described for facile, sensitive, rapid and selective monitoring of ALP. As a typical substrate for ALP detection, the p-nitrophenylphosphate (PNPP) was used to generate p-nitrophenol (PNP) through the catalysis of ALP. Interestingly, due to the specific hydrogen bond between PEI-CuNCs and PNP, the fluorescence of the PEI-CuNCs probe will be effectively quenched by the effect of PET. Meanwhile, because of the interaction between the unsaturated structure of polyethyleneimine and conjugate structure of PNP, the ultraviolet absorption of PNP will be strengthened via the hyperchromic effect. This fluorescent sensing strategy obtained the satisfactory linear range from 1 to 100 U/L with a LOD of 0.27 U/L as well as the absorption method of the system provided the linear range from 1 to 70 U/L with the LOD of 0.87 U/L. This dual-mode response in one detection platform can not only overcome the disadvantage of complicated modification process but also has an additional correction of the output signal.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI), p-nitrophenylphosphate (PNPP), ascorbic acid (AA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA), aminoase and amino acids were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. (Shanghai, China). Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), glucose (Glu), MgCl2, and other reagents were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Lysozyme (LYZ) was acquired from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The alkaline phosphatase (ALP, EC 3.1.3.1, 10 kU) from bovine intestinal mucosa and acetylcholinesterase (AChE, EC 3.1.1.7) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich (U.S.A.). A549 cells were obtained from Fujian medical university. All chemicals used were analytical grade without any further purification. Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane buffer solution (Tris-HCl, pH = 8.0) was prepared by mixing stock solutions of Tris and HCl. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm), which was obtained through a Millipore Milli-Q water system (Barnstead, CA, USA).

The UV–vis spectra were carried out on a Thermo Scientific microplate reader (Shanghai, China). The fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a Xenon lamp, and the slit widths were set to be 5.0 nm for both the excitation and emission. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was measured on a JEOL JEM-3010 microscope (Tokyo, Japan), which was operated at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Thermo Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Madison, USA). A549 cell imaging was recorded by Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM, Nikon C2, Japan).

In a typical assay with a slight modification [42], 500 mg PEI was dissolved in 4 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 30 min to facilitate the dissolution. Subsequently, 2 mL of CuSO4·5H2O (10 mM) and 100 μL of AA were added in the above solution under stirring. Then the mixed solution was stirred in a constant temperature water bath at 40 °C and reacted for about 8 h, and the final mixture is a clear and transparent green solution. Without any purification or centrifugation, the obtained PEI-Cu NCs were stored at room temperature before use.

The 50 μL of ALP with different activities (0–100 U/L) was added into the reaction system (Tris-HCl, pH = 8.0) with 10 mM PNPP and 0.1 μM MgCl2. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 40 min, and then 50 μL of PEI-Cu NCs were transferred to the reaction system, then the system was shaken slightly for 5 min to ensure the adequate reaction. The fluorescent spectra of the system with various concentrations of ALP were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, and the UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained by an automatic microplate reader.

To investigate the selectivity of the dual-mode sensor towards ALP activity, other nonspecific enzymes, proteins, metal ions and amino acids including AChE (10 μg/mL), LYZ (10 μg/mL), Aminoase (10 μg/mL), AA (10 mM), Glu (10 mM), BSA (10 μg/mL), HSA (10 μg/mL), FeCl2 (10 mM), ZnCl2 (10 mM), MgCl2 (10 mM), CaCl2 (10 mM), KCl (10 mM), NaCl (10 mM) and all the amino acids, Lysine (Lys, 1 mM), Valine (Val, 1 mM), Trptophan (Trp, 1 mM), Phenylalanine (Phe, 1 mM), Glycine (Gly, 1 mM), and Cysteine (Cys, 1 mM), were tested under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The determination of human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The serum samples were pretreated under the following procedures to eliminate impurity interference and to improve the recovery. Briefly, to removing the proteins in the serum samples, 3 mL of trichloroacetic acid (15 wt%) was added into 1 mL of serum samples and sonicating for 30 min. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Then taking the supernatant and modulated to pH 7.0 using NaOH solution. The obtained samples were diluted 20 times with ultrapure water, then various activities of ALP solutions were added to the final samples. The procedure of the ALP activity assay, as described in section 2.4.

To reveal the cytotoxic of PEI-Cu NCs, MTT assay was operated in this study. A549 cells were seeded in 96-well plates, and the density of cells in each well was counted as 104. These 96-well plates were placed in a humidified incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2) for 24 h. Subsequently, A549 cells were mixed in medium containing PEI-CuNCs with different relative standard dilution factors (0.1–1x), and medium without PEI-CuNCs was used as a control. After incubation for 24h, cell viability was obtained and calculated by standard MTT assay on a microplate reader. The whole experiment was repeated.

A549 cells were selected for endogenous ALP bio-imaging. Firstly, the A549 cells were plated overnight, and the density of cells in each well was counted as 105. Then, PEI-Cu NCs (0.2 x) and PEI-Cu NCs (0.2 x) with PNPP (10 mM) were respectively added into the petri dish. The mixture systems were incubated for 24 h. After washed with PBS, 5% paraformaldehyde was used to fix the cells for 30 min, then cleaned the cells by PBS 3 times and added the 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, 1 μg/mL) to stain the cells for 15 min. Finally, the fluorescence signals of PEI-Cu NCs from cells were collected by CLSM.

The as-prepared PEI-Cu NCs were characterized by TEM, UV–visible absorption spectra, fluorescence spectroscopy, and FT-IR spectroscopy, respectively. The average size and morphology of the obtained PEI-Cu NCs are shown in Fig. 1 A. From the TEM image, PEI-Cu NCs are all spherical in shape and excellent monodispersity in aqueous solution with about 3 nm in average size. As shown in the HRTEM image of Cu NCs (inserts in Fig. 1A), the crystal lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the Cu (111) facet [40]. The characteristic peak of Cu NCs is observed in UV–visible absorption spectra obviously at 350 nm (Fig. 1B). It can be seen in Fig. 1C that the PEI-Cu NCs has an emission peak around 500 nm under the 380 nm excitation. Meanwhile, the green emission from transparent PEI-Cu NCs solution is easily observed under UV illumination, indicating that fluorescent Cu NCs have been synthesized successfully. As presented in Fig. 1D, the FT-IR spectra of PEI-Cu NCs exhibits a broad, strong single band at 3600–3000 cm−1 and a slight peak at 1580 cm−1, indicating the structure of N–H. The dual absorption peaks of 2935 cm−1 and 2830 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of –CH2. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 1360 cm−1 can be regarded as a stretching vibration of C–N. These results reveal that the PEI-protected Cu NCs have been prepared in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of ALP activity assay based on PET and hyperchromic effect
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the process of fluorescent signal readout, The PEI-Cu NCs were used as the starting fluorophore. As a common ALP substrate, PNPP was chosen for the ALP assay for its ease to obtain excellent enzymatic utilization and good solubility in water. The overall strategy involved in devising the dual-mode signal for ALP assay is illustrated in Scheme 1 . PNPP can be catalyzed to produce PNP by the specific hydrolysis of ALP, and then the resultant PNP combines with PEI though the hydrogen bond interaction between phenolic hydroxyl and imine. Due to the nitryl possesses negative induction and conjugation effect for phenyl in PNP, then photoinduced electron transfer (PET) occurs between the PNP and PEI parts of the probe, where PNP and PEI act as electron acceptors and electron donors, respectively. The fluorescence of the system is then quenched. Meanwhile, a large conjugate structure is conformed between the unsaturated groups in PEI and phenyl in PNP, which causes the hyperchromic effect with the UV absorption enhancement at 405 nm for PNP. The dual-mode signal is related to the levels of PNP and ALP and can be used to establish a quantitative platform for the detection of ALP activity.

In order to verify this design, the different conditions of fluorescence and UV–visible absorption spectra were performed, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1, in the absence of ALP, the fluorescence emission intensity did not change, but decreased with the increase of ALP activity, indicating the promise sensitive of the design. The UV–visible absorption spectra of the PEI-Cu NCs, PNPP, PEI-Cu NCs + PNPP, and PEI-Cu NCs + ALP present no absorption at 405 nm. Interestingly, PNP exhibited a slight absorption, and the enhancement of absorption has been observed when PNP was added into PEI-Cu NCs. The same result can be recorded in PEI-Cu NCs, PNPP, and ALP system because of the generation of PNP (Fig. 2 ), which can achieve the determination of ALP by UV–visible absorption spectra. In order to explore possible interacting models between PEI-Cu NCs and PNP, the IR spectra are presented in Fig. S2. Different from the strong single band at 3400–3000 cm−1 in the PEI-Cu NCs spectrum, a slight broad band has been obtained at 3300–3000 cm−1 in PEI-Cu NCs and PNP system, revealing the interacting between them might be the hydrogen bond.

In order to establish a dual-mode detection method for ALP reaction, the related conditions were investigated in detail. First of all, considering its effect on PEI-Cu NCs and the stability of the reaction system including ALP, the appropriate pH value of the sensing system was determined. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence of PEI-Cu NCs shows good stability to the change of pH. However, with the different pH values, obvious fluorescence quenching can be observed in the system, especially when the pH values greater than 8.0, the value of F/F0 reaches the peak. Fig. 3B reveals that almost no change of F/F0 is discovered in the absence of ALP (black curve). On the contrary, in the presence of different concentrations of alkaline phosphatase, with the extension of hydrolysis time, the fluorescence ratio of F/F0 gradually increased and tended to the plateau. In particular, a higher concentration of ALP causes a larger change of the F/F0. In order to ensure the complete completion of enzymatic hydrolysis, the incubation time of 40 min is used for further study. In addition, the optimized diagrams of the PNPP concentration and temperature can be obtained in Fig. S3, and Fig. S4, the optimal PNPP concentration, and temperature are chosen as 10 mM and 37 °C, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The dual-mode assay of ALP activity
ce:section.ce:para: The linearity and detection limit of this sensing platform was studied under the above optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence emission of PEI-Cu NCs is quenched gradually by the PET as the ALP activity increases from 0 to 100 U/L. Fig. 4B shows the relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and ALP activity. An excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.996) is obtained between fluorescence intensity and ALP activity ranging from 1 to 100 U/L. The calculated LOD is 0.27 U/L based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. In addition, the UV–visible absorption method to detect ALP was also studied. As shown in Fig. 4C, the intensity of the absorption band centered on 405 nm increases with the increase of ALP activity. It is shown in Fig. 4D that relative absorbance values at 405 nm corresponding to various activities of ALP. A good linear relationship (R2 = 0.985) is found between absorbance values, and the activity of ALP was linear in the range of 1–70 U/L. The calculated LOD is 0.87 U/L based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Table S1 summarizes the comparison with the reported ALP detection methods, indicating that our advanced dual-mode method is not inferior to those methods. The proposed method is also compared with other representative Cu NCs-based sensors. As the popular strategies, MnO2 nanosheets with luminescent materials [43] and inner filter effect [44] are usually employed to construct sensing platform for ALP detection. However, these methods are vulnerable to matrix and background interference, which may affect the detection results. In the presented platform, another signal was introduced into the sensing system, which can not only greatly counteract the interference of the matrix, but also serve as a reference signal to make the detection results more accurate.

The selectivity of the developed method was performed to the evaluated impact of potential interference for ALP activity assay. The reaction platform was operated with several potential interferences, including several enzymes (AChE, LYZ, Aminoase), proteins (HSA, BSA), amino acids (Lys, Val, Trp, Phe, Gly, Cys), metal ions (K+, Na+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+), and common compounds (AA, Glu). As shown in Fig. S5, there is no significant change in relative fluorescence intensity after introducing other potential interferences into the detection system. Interference experiments show that the aforementioned substances have a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of Cu NCs, and show that the sensing method has high selectivity for ALP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay performances for ALP in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the reliability of this method in serum samples, the developed dual-mode biosensor was applied to the determination of ALP activity in human serum samples. The results analyzed by our standard addition method are shown in Table 1 . The accuracy of the method was evaluated by analyzing the recovery rate of ALP in serum samples. The recovery rate is 96.5%–109%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 3.24%–6.34%. In order to further study the accuracy of ALP detection, the traditional standard method based on PNPP was used to determine ALP in serum samples [45]. The results of ALP determined by this method are consistent with those of the standard determination based on PNPP. The results show that the method has good reliability and repeatability. Therefore, the sensor system has a good application prospect in the quantitative analysis of ALP in serum or other biological body fluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and endogenous ALP activity sensing in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, small molecule fluorescent probes have been used in cell imaging of ALP because of its good biocompatibility and luminescent properties [46,47], however, the complex steps of synthesis and purification make the sensing process time-consuming. Therefore, easily available nanomaterials with good fluorescent property have been favored for cell imaging. Fluorescent Cu NCs, as the powerful tools, are usually applied for in vitro detection, its application to the bio-imaging of endogenous ALP needs to be further studied.

Cytotoxicity is an important index to reveal whether PEI-Cu NCs has the potential of intracellular detection, so the MTT method is used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of PEI-Cu NCs. As shown in Fig. S6, compared with the control group, low concentration of PEI-Cu NCs had no significant effect on the survival rate of A549 cells. Even if the PEI-Cu NCs concentration is diluted to 0.8 times of the standard solution after 24 h, the viability of cells still keeps more than 85%, which indicates that PEI-Cu NCs possesses good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity and can be used for ALP sensing in living cells.

Taking A549 cells with high expression of ALP as a model, the practicability of this method in intracellular ALP biological imaging was analyzed and verified [48]. The fluorescence cell images were presented in Fig. 5 for ALP activity detection, DAPI, as a stain, was used to position the A549 cells. After cultured with PEI-Cu NCs for 24 h, A549 cells exhibit bright blue-green fluorescence (Fig. 5A), indicating the PEI-Cu NCs are internalized into A549 cells successfully and well-dispersed in the cytoplasm. However, when the mixture of PNPP (10 mM) and PEI-Cu NCs incubate with cells, the fluorescence intensity of A549 cells decreased significantly, which can be observed easily in the green channel (Fig. 5B), because the PNPP can be hydrolyzed to PNP by endogenous ALP in cells, and as a quenching agent, PNP can weaken the fluorescence intensity of PEI-Cu NCs. These results indicate the feasibility of the sensor platform in the monitoring of intracellular ALP activity and can give more insight for other endogenous detection.

In summary, we describe a cheap and sensitive dual-mode analysis method of fluorescence and UV–vis absorption to detect the activity of ALP by using PET and hyperchromic effect. In this assay, Due to the nitryl possesses negative induction effect and negative conjugation effect for phenyl in PNP, PET and hyperchromic effect were achieved between the PNP and PEI-Cu NCs. On this basis, the dual-mode detection of alkaline phosphatase by fluorescence and UV–vis absorption was realized. Due to ALP can hydrolyze PNPP to produce PNP, and with the help of PNP, the activity of ALP has been detected selectively and sensitively. Moreover, this platform was applied to monitor ALP levels of serum and image the endogenous ALP in living cells, and the obtained results were satisfactory. Given its ease of operation and cost-effectiveness, this dual-mode assay based on electronic effect has a good prospect for the detection of ALP in biological systems. Furthermore, this established platform provides a new horizon into the application of Cu NCs to construct facile and economy sensors.

Xuemin Huang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Maojin Lan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation. Jian Wang: Investigation. Longhua Guo: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Zhenyu Lin: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Fan Zhang: Supervision. Tao Zhang: Supervision. Cuimin Wu: Supervision, Methodology. Bin Qiu: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of STS Key Project of Fujian Province (2017T3007), Nature Sciences Funding of Fujian Province (2018J01682). The Major Project of Fujian Province (2015YZ0003-1); The Construction Project of Science and Technology Innovation Platform in Fuzhou Health and Family Planning System (2018-S-wp2,2017-S-wp1 and 2018-S-wp3), the Major Science and Technology Projects of Fuzhou (2017), the Key Clinical Specialty Discipline Construction Program of Fujian Province (Orthopedics of Fuzhou Second Hospital).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.09.059.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of CXCR4 siRNA/dextran-spermine nanoparticles on CXCR4 expression and serum LDH levels in a mouse model of colorectal cancer metastasis to the liver

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Abedini, M. Ismail, H. Hosseinkhani, T.A.T. Ibrahim, A.R. Omar, P.P. Chong, M.H. Bejo, A.J. Domb, Effects of CXCR4 siRNA/dextran-spermine nanoparticles on CXCR4 expression and serum LDH levels in a mouse model of colorectal cancer metastasis to the liver, Cancer Management & Research, 3 (2011) 301-309.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hepatitis G virus RNA in the serum of patients with elevated gamma glutamyl transpeptidase and alkaline phosphatase: a specific liver disease? [corrected]

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Colombatto, A. Randone, G. Civitico, G.J. Monti, L. Dolci, N. Medaina, F. Oliveri, G. Verme, G. Marchiaro, R. Pagni, Hepatitis G virus RNA in the serum of patients with elevated gamma glutamyl transpeptidase and alkaline phosphatase: a specific liver disease? [corrected], Journal of Viral Hepatitis, 3 (1996) 301-306.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Serum bone alkaline phosphatase levels enhance the clinical utility of prostate specific antigen in the staging of newly diagnosed prostate cancer patients

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.A. Lorente, H. Valenzuela, J. Morote, A. Gelabert, Serum bone alkaline phosphatase levels enhance the clinical utility of prostate specific antigen in the staging of newly diagnosed prostate cancer patients, European Journal of Nuclear Medicine, 26 (1999) 625-632.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: BMP-2 controls alkaline phosphatase expression and osteoblast mineralization by a wnt autocrine loop

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Rawadi, B. Vayssiere, F. Dunn, R. Baron, S. Roman-Roman, BMP-2 Controls Alkaline Phosphatase Expression and Osteoblast Mineralization by a Wnt Autocrine Loop, Journal of Bone & Mineral Research, 18 (2003) 1842-1853.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: UV-nanoimprint lithography as a tool to develop flexible microfluidic devices for electrochemical detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chen, Y. Zhou, D. Wang, F. He, V.M. Rotello, K.R. Carter, J.J. Watkins, S.R. Nugen, UV-nanoimprint lithography as a tool to develop flexible microfluidic devices for electrochemical detection, Lab on A Chip, 15 (2015) 3086-3094.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzymatic hydrogelation-induced fluorescence turn-off for sensing alkaline phosphatase in vitro and in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Dong, Q. Miao, Z. Hai, Y. Yuan, G. Liang, Enzymatic Hydrogelation-Induced Fluorescence Turn-Off for Sensing Alkaline Phosphatase in Vitro and in Living Cells, Analytical Chemistry, 87 (2015) 6475-6478.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and sensitive fluorescence sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity with photoluminescent carbon dots based on inner filter effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Li, H. Fu, X. Chen, P. Gong, G. Chen, L. Xia, H. Wang, J.M. You, Y. Wu, Facile and Sensitive Fluorescence Sensing of Alkaline Phosphatase Activity with Photoluminescent Carbon Dots Based on Inner Filter Effect, Analytical Chemistry, 88 (2016) 2720-2726.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase by dephosphorylation-initiated transcription reaction-mediated dual signal amplification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Ma, W.J. Liu, L. Liang, B. Tang, C.Y. Zhang, Sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase by dephosphorylation-initiated transcription reaction-mediated dual signal amplification, Chemical Communications, 54 (2018) 2413-2416.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Qu, H. Pei, R. Kong, S. Zhu, L. Xia, Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets, Talanta, 165 (2016) 136-142.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent probe for alkaline phosphatase based on betaine-modified polyethylenimine via excimer/monomer conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Zheng, S. Guo, F. Zeng, J. Li, S. Wu, Ratiometric fluorescent probe for alkaline phosphatase based on betaine-modified polyethylenimine via excimer/monomer conversion, Analytical Chemistry, 86 (2014) 9873-9879.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer immunoassay for alkaline phosphatase using p-nitrophenyl phosphate as substrate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Qi, Y. Fu, M. Zhao, H. He, X. Tian, L. Hu, Y. Zhang, Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer immunoassay for alkaline phosphatase using p-nitrophenyl phosphate as substrate, Analytica Chimica Acta, 1097 (2020) 71-77.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Instrument-free quantitative detection of alkaline phosphatase using paper-based devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, J. Nie, H. Wang, J. Yang, B. Wang, Y. Zhang, J. Li, Instrument-free quantitative detection of alkaline phosphatase using paper-based devices, Analytical Methods, 9 (2017) 3375-3379.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence immunoassay based on the phosphate-triggered fluorescence turn-on detection of alkaline phosphatase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Chen, J. Zhao, Y. Lu, S. Jian, X. Yang, Fluorescence Immunoassay Based on the Phosphate-Triggered Fluorescence Turn-on Detection of Alkaline Phosphatase, Analytical Chemistry, 90 (2018) 3505-3511.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Fang, X.Q. Li, H. Wang, X.M. Wu, G.L. Wang, Tuning surface states to achieve the modulated fluorescence of carbon dots for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase and immunoassay of α-fetoprotein, Sensors & Actuators B Chemical, 257 (2018) 620-628.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Xiao, J. Sun, J. Zhao, S. Wang, G. Liu, X. Yang, FRET Effect between Fluorescent Polydopamine Nanoparticles and MnO2 Nanosheets and Its Application for Sensitive Sensing of Alkaline Phosphatase, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, 10 (2018) 6560-6569.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Q. Zhao, J.C. Lu, Y.H. Yuan, T. Cong, J.S. Jia, R.C. Jian, F. Hui, A real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on carbon quantum dots, Biosensors & Bioelectronics, 68 (2015) 675-680.

J. Zheng, C. Zhou, M. Yu, J. Liu, Different sized luminescent gold nanoparticles, Nanoscale, 4 (2012) 4073-4083.
